questions.stem: Which interpretation should guide the nurse's next move when this client becomes increasingly paranoid and agitated?
questions.scenario: A client with schizophrenia is pacing, shouting that staff members are trying to poison him, and clenching his fists.
questions.additionalInfo: The unit is noisy, and several other clients have gathered nearby to watch the scene.

options.text: This is a safety and de-escalation situation that calls for a calm, low-stimulation approach and removal of excess triggers
The nurse should argue with the delusion to prove the belief is false
The nurse should bring more clients close by to normalize the interaction
The nurse should leave immediately without setting limits or assessing risk

questions.rationale: Acute psychosis with rising agitation should be answered with safety, lower stimulation, and calm limit setting rather than confrontation. Arguing about the delusion or adding more external stimulation increases the risk that the client's distress escalates into violence.

distractorRationales.b: The safer response addresses clinical safety risk; "The nurse should argue with the delusion to prove the belief is false" moves care toward diagnostic reassurance before bedside safety action.
distractorRationales.c: The safer response addresses clinical safety risk; "The nurse should bring more clients close by to normalize the interaction" moves care toward reassurance before bedside safety action.
distractorRationales.d: Because acute psychosis with rising agitation should be answered with safety, lower stimulation suggests clinical safety risk, "The nurse should leave immediately without setting limits or assessing risk" would not correct the active problem. Nursing safety depends on matching the response to the active clinical risk before routine care.

questions.takeaway: In psychosis, the nurse leads with safety and calm structure, not debate.
questions.speedCue: Paranoia plus clenched fists means lower the room before you lower the delusion.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: Which interpretation should guide the nurse's next move when family demands conflict with a documented client directive?
questions.scenario: A client with metastatic cancer has a signed advance directive declining intubation and CPR.
questions.additionalInfo: The client is now unresponsive, and two adult children insist the nurse should 'do everything' while the provider is en route.

options.text: Honor the documented advance directive while escalating the conflict through the provider and ethics chain
Ignore the directive because family requests automatically override prior written wishes
Delay all action until the family agrees on a plan
Ask the family to sign a new code-status form at the bedside before doing anything else

questions.rationale: A valid advance directive reflects the client's expressed wishes and should guide care when the client can no longer speak. The nurse should follow that documented plan while escalating the family conflict through the proper chain rather than treating family pressure as a replacement for the client's autonomy.

distractorRationales.b: Because advance directive conflict family safety suggests circulation or perfusion, "Ignore the directive because family requests automatically override prior written wishes" would not correct the active problem. Perfusion problems require rapid assessment and stabilization before comfort measures or delayed provider follow-up.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor shifts care toward delayed assessment instead of circulation and perfusion support. The stem cue "advance directive conflict family safety" makes circulation or perfusion the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave circulation or perfusion untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Honor the documented advance directive while escalating the conflict through the provider and ethics chain" fits the safer response to advance directive conflict family safety.

questions.takeaway: When family demands conflict with a valid directive, the client's documented wishes stay at the center of care.
questions.speedCue: The shortcut is autonomy first: a signed directive is the client's voice when the family is distressed.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: Which interpretation should guide the nurse's next move after this abnormal gestational-diabetes screen?
questions.scenario: A pregnant client at 28 weeks has an abnormal glucose screening result and says, 'I only feel thirsty because I'm pregnant.'
questions.additionalInfo: The client asks whether anything really needs to change if the baby is moving normally.

options.text: This result needs follow-up teaching and blood-glucose management planning because untreated hyperglycemia raises maternal and fetal risk
The result is expected in pregnancy and does not need any follow-up unless contractions begin
The client should stop all carbohydrates immediately until the next prenatal visit
Fetal movement alone rules out meaningful glucose-related risk

questions.rationale: An abnormal gestational-diabetes screen needs follow-up and counseling because hyperglycemia affects both maternal and fetal outcomes. The nurse should frame this as ongoing health-promotion and risk-reduction work, not as a benign pregnancy symptom pattern.

distractorRationales.b: "The result is expected in pregnancy and does not need any follow-up unless contractions begin" misses the bedside risk because an abnormal gestational-diabetes screen needs follow-up and counseling because hyperglycemia affects both maternal and fetal outcomes points to unstable glucose physiology. Glucose instability can progress quickly, so medication timing and metabolic rescue come before routine follow-up.
distractorRationales.c: "The client should stop all carbohydrates immediately until the next prenatal visit" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "An abnormal gestational-diabetes screen needs follow-up and counseling because hyperglycemia affects both maternal and fetal outcomes" raises concern for unstable glucose physiology, so glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.d: "Fetal movement alone rules out meaningful glucose-related risk" misses the bedside risk because an abnormal gestational-diabetes screen needs follow-up and counseling because hyperglycemia affects both maternal and fetal outcomes points to unstable glucose physiology. Glucose instability can progress quickly, so medication timing and metabolic rescue come before routine follow-up.

questions.takeaway: An abnormal gestational-diabetes screen is a management issue, not a reassurance cue.
questions.speedCue: Normal fetal movement does not cancel a maternal glucose problem.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: Which interpretation should guide the nurse's next move with this back-pain presentation?
questions.scenario: A client with severe low-back pain reports new urinary retention, numbness in the inner thighs, and increasing leg weakness.
questions.additionalInfo: The client asks whether this is just another sciatica flare and whether walking it off would help.

options.text: Cauda equina syndrome requiring urgent spinal decompression evaluation
Routine lumbar strain that should improve with activity
Expected opioid side effects causing harmless bladder hesitancy
An anxiety response to chronic back pain without neurologic danger

questions.rationale: New urinary retention, saddle anesthesia symptoms, and weakness create a cauda-equina map. The nurse should answer from cord-compression urgency, not from routine back-pain thinking.

distractorRationales.b: "Routine lumbar strain that should improve with activity" misses the bedside risk because bladder retention and saddle numbness are the clues that make back pain urgent points to clinical safety risk. Maternal-fetal risk requires immediate safety measures because fetal oxygenation and maternal perfusion can deteriorate quickly.
distractorRationales.c: "Expected opioid side effects causing harmless bladder hesitancy" normalizes a finding that needs reassessment. The cue "The nurse should answer from cord-compression urgency, not from routine back-pain thinking" raises concern for clinical safety risk, so bedside safety action is needed before reassurance.
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses clinical safety risk; "An anxiety response to chronic back pain without neurologic danger" moves care toward diagnostic reassurance before bedside safety action.

questions.takeaway: Bladder retention and saddle numbness are the clues that make back pain urgent.
questions.speedCue: Back pain plus retention plus saddle symptoms is the shortcut.

questions.stem: Which interpretation best explains this client's sudden change after fracture repair?
questions.scenario: A client with a femur fracture becomes acutely confused and hypoxic 24 hours after injury, and a faint petechial rash appears on the chest.
questions.additionalInfo: The nurse is deciding whether to frame this as anxiety, pneumonia, or a fracture-related emergency.

options.text: Fat embolism syndrome requiring urgent oxygenation-focused escalation
Simple postoperative anxiety without true pulmonary danger
Expected pain-related tachypnea after fracture stabilization
Minor skin irritation causing an unrelated rash

questions.rationale: Long-bone fracture followed by hypoxia, confusion, and petechiae creates a fat-embolism map. The nurse should answer from respiratory and neurologic decline, not from pain or benign rash thinking.

distractorRationales.b: Because the nurse should answer from respiratory and neurologic decline, not from pain or benign rash thinking suggests oxygenation or ventilation, "Simple postoperative anxiety without true pulmonary danger" would not correct the active problem. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.
distractorRationales.c: Because the nurse should answer from respiratory and neurologic decline, not from pain or benign rash thinking suggests oxygenation or ventilation, "Expected pain-related tachypnea after fracture stabilization" would not correct the active problem. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave oxygenation or ventilation untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Fat embolism syndrome requiring urgent oxygenation-focused escalation" fits the safer response to the nurse is deciding whether to frame this as anxiety, pneumonia, or a fracture-related emergency.

questions.takeaway: Hypoxia plus confusion plus petechiae is the clue cluster to find fast.
questions.speedCue: Long bone fracture plus petechiae plus hypoxia is the shortcut.

questions.stem: Which interpretation should guide the nurse's next move with this endocrine collapse pattern?
questions.scenario: An older adult is brought in with hypothermia, bradycardia, slow respirations, and declining mental status.
questions.additionalInfo: Family members say the client has known hypothyroidism and has recently seemed increasingly slowed and withdrawn.

options.text: Myxedema coma requiring airway, warming, and urgent endocrine-focused escalation
Expected age-related slowing without a true emergency
A minor viral illness that should improve with rest alone
A routine sedative effect that can be reassessed later

questions.rationale: Hypothermia, bradycardia, hypoventilation, and mental-status decline in a client with hypothyroidism create a myxedema-coma map. The nurse should answer from endocrine collapse and airway risk, not from general weakness or benign slowing.

distractorRationales.b: This distractor shifts care toward false reassurance instead of airway and breathing intervention. The stem cue "An older adult is brought in with hypothermia, bradycardia, slow respirations" makes oxygenation or ventilation the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.c: The option can leave oxygenation or ventilation untreated because it commits to a competing cause before checking the dangerous pattern. "Myxedema coma requiring airway, warming, and urgent endocrine-focused escalation" fits the safer response to profound slowing plus hypothermia is the clue cluster that makes hypothyroidism emergent.
distractorRationales.d: Because the nurse should answer from endocrine collapse and airway risk, not from general weakness or benign slowing suggests oxygenation or ventilation, "A routine sedative effect that can be reassessed later" would not correct the active problem. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.

questions.takeaway: Profound slowing plus hypothermia is the clue cluster that makes hypothyroidism emergent.
questions.speedCue: Cold, slow, and less responsive is the shortcut.

questions.stem: Which interpretation should guide the nurse's next move in this hemodynamic decline?
questions.scenario: A client recovering from cardiac surgery develops muffled heart sounds, jugular venous distention, narrowing pulse pressure, and worsening hypotension.
questions.additionalInfo: Urine output is falling and the client becomes more anxious with each reassessment.

options.text: Pericardial tamponade impairing ventricular filling and requiring urgent cardiac escalation
Routine postoperative pain causing temporary vital-sign changes
Simple fluid overload that only needs a diuretic first
Expected anxiety after surgery without true perfusion compromise

questions.rationale: Muffled heart sounds, JVD, and hypotension create a tamponade map because the heart is failing to fill effectively. The nurse should answer from obstructed cardiac output and urgent escalation, not from pain or anxiety assumptions.

distractorRationales.b: This distractor shifts care toward delayed assessment instead of renal protection. The stem cue "Urine output is falling and the client becomes more anxious with each reassessment" makes renal injury the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.c: The safer response addresses renal injury; "Simple fluid overload that only needs a diuretic first" moves care toward routine care before renal protection.
distractorRationales.d: "Expected anxiety after surgery without true perfusion compromise" normalizes a finding that needs reassessment. The cue "hypotension create a tamponade map because the heart is failing to fill effectively" raises concern for renal injury, so renal protection is needed before reassurance.

questions.takeaway: Low pressure with JVD after cardiac surgery should make filling failure feel immediate.
questions.speedCue: JVD plus muffled tones plus hypotension is the shortcut.

questions.stem: Which interpretation should guide the nurse's next move in this infarction-related hypotension?
questions.scenario: A client with an inferior STEMI becomes more hypotensive after receiving nitroglycerin.
questions.additionalInfo: The client has clear lungs, cool skin, and rising jugular venous pressure.

options.text: Right ventricular infarction with preload dependence, making nitrates poorly tolerated
Expected medication relaxation without perfusion significance
Simple dehydration that only needs oral fluids
A benign anxiety episode triggered by chest pain

questions.rationale: Inferior MI plus hypotension after nitrates with clear lungs suggests a right-sided infarction that needs preload. The nurse should answer from hemodynamic dependence, not from generic antianginal expectations.

distractorRationales.b: "Expected medication relaxation without perfusion significance" normalizes a finding that needs reassessment. The cue "Hypotension after nitrates with clear lungs is a clue that preload matters" raises concern for circulation or perfusion, so circulation and perfusion support is needed before reassurance.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor shifts care toward premature activity instead of circulation and perfusion support. The stem cue "Hypotension after nitrates with clear lungs is a clue that preload matters" makes circulation or perfusion the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.d: "A benign anxiety episode triggered by chest pain" misses the bedside risk because inferior MI plus hypotension after nitrates with clear lungs suggests a right-sided infarction that needs preload points to circulation or perfusion. Perfusion problems require rapid assessment and stabilization before comfort measures or delayed provider follow-up.

questions.takeaway: Hypotension after nitrates with clear lungs is a clue that preload matters.
questions.speedCue: Inferior MI plus nitrate drop plus JVD is the shortcut.

questions.stem: Which decision map best explains the nurse's priority after recognizing that the wrong dose of insulin was given?
questions.scenario: The nurse discovers that 10 units of rapid-acting insulin were administered instead of the prescribed 4 units.
questions.additionalInfo: The client is awake but now diaphoretic and shaky, and the blood glucose result is pending.

options.text: Treat the client as the priority, assess and intervene for hypoglycemia, notify the provider, then complete the reporting chain
Complete the incident report before assessing the client so the timeline is accurate
Wait for symptoms to worsen so the provider has clear evidence of harm
Ask the next nurse to decide whether the error needs to be disclosed

questions.rationale: After a medication error, the unstable client comes before paperwork. The nurse should assess and treat the potential hypoglycemia first, notify the provider, and then complete the required reporting process rather than allowing documentation to delay urgent care.

distractorRationales.b: The safer response addresses unstable glucose physiology; "Complete the incident report before assessing the client so the timeline is accurate" moves care toward routine care before glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization.
distractorRationales.c: The safer response addresses unstable glucose physiology; "Wait for symptoms to worsen so the provider has clear evidence of harm" moves care toward routine observation before glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization.
distractorRationales.d: "Ask the next nurse to decide whether the error needs to be disclosed" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "The nurse should assess and treat the potential hypoglycemia first, notify the provider" raises concern for unstable glucose physiology, so glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization is needed before routine care.

questions.takeaway: Reporting matters, but the harmed or unstable client always comes first.
questions.speedCue: Error chain shortcut: patient first, provider second, paperwork third.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: Which decision map best explains the nurse's next move in this airway decline?
questions.scenario: A client with myasthenia gravis develops increasing dysphagia, weak cough, and shallow respirations after a respiratory infection.
questions.additionalInfo: The client can barely count to 10 before stopping for breath and now sounds exhausted while speaking.

options.text: Myasthenic crisis with impending ventilatory failure requiring airway-focused escalation
Simple anxiety that only needs reassurance and breathing coaching
Expected fatigue from poor sleep without respiratory danger
A routine medication-timing issue that can wait for the next dose

questions.rationale: Bulbar weakness, weak cough, and shallow breathing in myasthenia gravis create an airway-failure map. The nurse should answer from ventilatory rescue and urgent escalation, not from reassurance or routine scheduling logic.

distractorRationales.b: "Simple anxiety that only needs reassurance and breathing coaching" moves to activity, intake, or discharge planning too early. The cue "Weak cough and swallowing trouble are the clues that make this a breathing question" raises concern for oxygenation or ventilation, so airway and breathing intervention is needed before ambulation, intake, or discharge teaching.
distractorRationales.c: The safer response addresses oxygenation or ventilation; "Expected fatigue from poor sleep without respiratory danger" moves care toward reassurance before airway and breathing intervention.
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses oxygenation or ventilation; "A routine medication-timing issue that can wait for the next dose" moves care toward routine observation before airway and breathing intervention.

questions.takeaway: Weak cough and swallowing trouble are the clues that make this a breathing question.
questions.speedCue: Myasthenia plus bulbar weakness plus fatigue breathing is the shortcut to crisis thinking.

questions.stem: Which decision map best explains the nurse's next move in this oncology emergency?
questions.scenario: A client 7 days after chemotherapy has a temperature of 38.6 C (101.5 F), chills, and an absolute neutrophil count of 300.
questions.additionalInfo: Blood pressure is still normal, but the client looks ill and asks whether acetaminophen alone is enough.

options.text: Neutropenic fever requiring sepsis-focused escalation, cultures, and prompt broad-spectrum coverage
Expected chemotherapy flu-like symptoms needing only rest and fluids
A minor temperature change that can wait until morning rounds
A viral syndrome that should be managed with visitors and fresh fruit

questions.rationale: Fever plus profound neutropenia creates a sepsis-risk map even before obvious shock appears. The nurse should answer from rapid escalation and infection workup, not from routine fever comfort care.

distractorRationales.b: "Expected chemotherapy flu-like symptoms needing only rest and fluids" misses the bedside risk because fever plus profound neutropenia creates a sepsis-risk map even before obvious shock appears points to infection progression. Infection control or sepsis escalation limits spread and organ hypoperfusion before lower-acuity care.
distractorRationales.c: "A minor temperature change that can wait until morning rounds" treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. The cue "6 C (101" raises concern for infection progression, so infection or sepsis escalation is needed before routine observation.
distractorRationales.d: Because 6 C (101 suggests infection progression, "A viral syndrome that should be managed with visitors and fresh fruit" would not correct the active problem. Infection control or sepsis escalation limits spread and organ hypoperfusion before lower-acuity care.

questions.takeaway: Profound neutropenia turns fever into an emergency even when the pressure looks okay.
questions.speedCue: Fever plus ANC 300 is the shortcut to sepsis-level urgency.

questions.stem: Which decision map should guide the nurse's response to this rapid deterioration?

questions.additionalInfo: The client becomes increasingly restless and the monitor shows falling oxygen saturation despite supplemental oxygen.

options.text: Tension pneumothorax causing obstructive shock and requiring immediate decompression-focused escalation
Routine post-procedure pain that only needs an analgesic
Expected atelectasis that can wait for incentive spirometry
An anxiety episode without a true ventilation problem

questions.stem: Which decision map best explains this client's rapid deterioration?
questions.scenario: A client with chronic adrenal insufficiency has been vomiting for 24 hours and now looks weak and confused.

options.text: Adrenal crisis requiring immediate steroid replacement, fluid support, and shock-focused escalation
Simple dehydration that only needs oral fluids
Expected fatigue from chronic illness without emergency risk
A benign stomach virus that can wait for clinic follow-up

questions.stem: Which clinical map should guide the nurse's next move after this transfusion starts?

distractorRationales.d: "A minor symptom set that should be charted while the blood continues" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "Chills, low-back pain" raises concern for circulation or perfusion, so circulation and perfusion support is needed before routine care.

questions.stem: Which decision map best explains this change after fracture care?

options.text: Compartment syndrome requiring urgent pressure-relief escalation and neurovascular protection
Expected postoperative pain that can wait for the next dose of medication

questions.stem: Which findings in this febrile client support the nurse's concern for meningococcal sepsis? Select all that apply.
questions.scenario: A college student arrives with fever, severe headache, malaise, and rapidly worsening illness.
questions.additionalInfo: The nurse is deciding which additional findings would strengthen concern for a meningococcal emergency rather than a routine viral illness.

options.text: A nonblanching petechial or purpuric rash
Hypotension or rapidly worsening perfusion
Neck stiffness and photophobia
A mild itchy rash that fades with pressure
Warm, stable vitals with improving symptoms after fluids
Acute confusion or worsening mental status

questions.rationale: Meningococcal sepsis clues cluster around invasive infection and rapid deterioration: nonblanching rash, perfusion decline, meningeal irritation, and mental-status change. The nurse should exclude benign or improving patterns that do not fit invasive sepsis.

distractorRationales.d: "A mild itchy rash that fades with pressure" normalizes a finding that needs reassessment. The cue "Meningococcal sepsis clues cluster around invasive infection and rapid deterioration: nonblanching rash, perfusion decline, meningeal irritation" raises concern for infection progression, so infection or sepsis escalation is needed before reassurance.
distractorRationales.e: The safer response addresses infection progression; "Warm, stable vitals with improving symptoms after fluids" moves care toward diagnostic reassurance before infection or sepsis escalation.

questions.takeaway: The fast clue is not fever alone, but fever plus rash and collapse signs.
questions.speedCue: Nonblanching rash plus neuro change or shock is the shortcut.

questions.stem: Which findings make severe symptomatic hyponatremia the best interpretation? Select all that apply.

Seizure activity after worsening mentation

Normal orientation and steady gait

questions.stem: Which findings support the nurse's concern that this newborn is becoming hypoglycemic? Select all that apply.
questions.scenario: A newborn of a gestational-diabetic parent is 3 hours old and is becoming harder to feed.
questions.additionalInfo: The nurse is deciding whether the bedside change fits a glucose problem or a routine transition period.

options.text: Jitteriness with tremulous extremities
Weak or poor feeding effort
Low axillary temperature
Strong lusty cry with vigorous rooting
Pink color and normal spontaneous wakefulness

questions.rationale: Newborn hypoglycemia often shows up as jitteriness, poor feeding, and temperature instability. The nurse should cluster the subtle findings together quickly instead of waiting for a dramatic crash.

distractorRationales.d: Because the nurse is deciding whether the bedside change fits a glucose problem or a routine transition period suggests unstable glucose physiology, "Strong lusty cry with vigorous rooting" would not correct the active problem. Glucose instability can progress quickly, so medication timing and metabolic rescue come before routine follow-up.
distractorRationales.e: The option can leave unstable glucose physiology untreated because it normalizes a finding that needs reassessment. "Jitteriness with tremulous extremities plus Weak or poor feeding effort plus Low axillary temperature" fits the safer response to newborn hypoglycemia often shows up as jitteriness, poor feeding.

questions.takeaway: In newborns, multiple small instability clues often matter more than one dramatic sign.
questions.speedCue: Jitteriness plus poor feeding should move glucose near the top immediately.

visualRationale.conclusion: In newborns, multiple small instability clues often matter more than one dramatic sign.

questions.stem: A client in labor has a sudden fetal heart rate drop after rupture of membranes and the nurse sees a loop of umbilical cord. Which immediate actions should the nurse take?
questions.scenario: The fetal heart rate remains in the 70s while help is being called.

options.text: Use a gloved hand to lift the presenting part off the cord
Position the client to relieve pressure on the cord
Attempt to push the visible cord back into the vagina
Delay intervention until the provider is at the bedside
Call for immediate obstetric assistance
Start coached pushing with the next contraction

questions.rationale: Cord prolapse is a compression emergency. The nurse's first moves are to relieve the pressure, position the client to reduce compression, and summon immediate obstetric help. Pushing the cord back or delaying action increases fetal hypoxia risk.

distractorRationales.c: "Attempt to push the visible cord back into the vagina" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "Pushing the cord back or delaying action increases fetal hypoxia risk" raises concern for circulation or perfusion, so circulation and perfusion support is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.d: "Delay intervention until the provider is at the bedside" misses the bedside risk because pushing the cord back or delaying action increases fetal hypoxia risk points to circulation or perfusion. Perfusion problems require rapid assessment and stabilization before comfort measures or delayed provider follow-up.
distractorRationales.f: This distractor shifts care toward premature attribution instead of circulation and perfusion support. The stem cue "The fetal heart rate remains in the 70s while help is being called" makes circulation or perfusion the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: Cord prolapse means relieve compression now, not when the provider arrives.
questions.speedCue: If you can see the cord and the heart rate crashes, think pressure-off-the-cord immediately.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A client with profuse diarrhea tests positive for C. difficile. Place the nursing actions in the order the nurse should take them.
questions.scenario: The nurse receives the result just before transferring the client to a semi-private room.
questions.additionalInfo: The unit has gowns, gloves, soap-and-water handwashing sinks, and sporicidal disinfectant available.

options.text: Initiate contact-plus-enteric precautions with gown and gloves
Place the client in an appropriate room and dedicate equipment when possible
Perform soap-and-water hand hygiene after care
Ensure the environment is cleaned with a sporicidal product

questions.rationale: C. difficile prevention works as a bundle: start precautions, protect room placement and equipment, use soap-and-water hand hygiene, and ensure sporicidal cleaning. The sequence keeps spores from spreading while also protecting the next patient environment.
questions.takeaway: For C. difficile, think gloves and gown, room protection, soap-and-water, then sporicidal cleaning.
questions.speedCue: The shortcut is spores: alcohol gel alone is not enough.

visualRationale.title: C_diff_transmission_prevention_bundle
visualRationale.caption: For C. difficile, think gloves and gown, room protection, soap-and-water, then sporicidal cleaning.
visualRationale.conclusion: For C. difficile, think gloves and gown, room protection, soap-and-water, then sporicidal cleaning.

questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: Place the nursing actions in the order that best protects the client before the first enteral feeding starts.
questions.scenario: A newly inserted small-bore feeding tube is in place, and the provider has written the order to begin feedings.
questions.additionalInfo: The nurse is preparing the bedside setup while waiting for placement confirmation.

options.text: Confirm the prescribed placement-verification method has been completed
Position the client with the head of bed elevated
Flush the tube per protocol after confirmation
Begin the feeding only after placement and position are safe

questions.rationale: Before the first feeding, the nurse verifies safe placement, protects aspiration risk with positioning, flushes per protocol, and only then starts the feeding. Starting nutrition before location is confirmed creates avoidable airway harm.
questions.takeaway: The first enteral feed starts only after safe placement is confirmed.
questions.speedCue: Placement before pouring formula is the shortcut.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: For each postoperative task, click to specify whether the task should stay with the RN or can be delegated to the UAP.
questions.scenario: The RN is caring for several postoperative clients and is working with an experienced UAP.
questions.additionalInfo: The RN must decide which work can safely move and which work must stay with licensed nursing judgment.

options.text: Keep with RN
Delegate to UAP

matrixRows.label: Assess a client with new shortness of breath two hours after surgery
Assist a stable client to the bathroom for the first time after the RN has assessed
Reinforce coughing and deep-breathing after the RN provides the initial teaching
Evaluate a new report of uncontrolled incisional pain

questions.rationale: Delegation becomes faster when the nurse separates stable, predictable tasks from new assessment or evaluation needs. Acute respiratory change and uncontrolled pain require RN judgment, while routine assistance and reinforcement after assessment can move safely to the UAP.

distractorRationales.a: "Keep with RN" commits to a competing cause before checking the dangerous pattern. The cue "while routine assistance and reinforcement after assessment can move safely to the UAP" raises concern for oxygenation or ventilation, so airway and breathing intervention is needed before diagnostic reassurance.
distractorRationales.b: "Delegate to UAP" misses the bedside risk because while routine assistance and reinforcement after assessment can move safely to the UAP points to oxygenation or ventilation. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.

questions.takeaway: New problems stay with the RN; stable, routine assistance can move.
questions.speedCue: Ask whether the task needs fresh assessment or evaluation. If yes, it stays with the RN.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: For each arterial blood gas pattern, click to specify whether the pattern is primarily respiratory or primarily metabolic.
questions.scenario: The nurse is triaging several ABG reports and needs to sort the primary problem quickly before acting.
questions.additionalInfo: Each row represents the main disturbance, not the final compensation story.

options.text: Primarily respiratory
Primarily metabolic

matrixRows.label: pH 7.29, PaCO2 58 mm Hg, HCO3 26 mEq/L
pH 7.30, PaCO2 31 mm Hg, HCO3 16 mEq/L
pH 7.50, PaCO2 30 mm Hg, HCO3 24 mEq/L
pH 7.48, PaCO2 42 mm Hg, HCO3 31 mEq/L

questions.rationale: Fast ABG interpretation starts by deciding which value is pushing the pH out of range. Elevated or low carbon dioxide points toward respiratory causes, while an abnormal bicarbonate driving the pH change points toward metabolic causes.

distractorRationales.a: The safer response addresses oxygenation or ventilation; "Primarily respiratory" moves care toward routine care before airway and breathing intervention.
distractorRationales.b: "Primarily metabolic" misses the bedside risk because find what is moving the pH first points to oxygenation or ventilation. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.

questions.takeaway: Find what is moving the pH first; compensation is the second step.
questions.speedCue: pH first, then match the value pushing it.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A client with profuse diarrhea tests positive for Clostridioides difficile. Which infection-control action should the nurse implement?

options.text: Use soap and water for hand hygiene after removing gloves.
Use alcohol-based sanitizer alone because it is faster.
Place the client on droplet precautions only.
Share the blood pressure cuff after wiping it with a dry towel.

questions.rationale: C. difficile spores are not reliably removed by alcohol-based hand rub alone, so soap-and-water hand hygiene is required after glove removal. Contact precautions and dedicated equipment are also important. Using only sanitizer or shared equipment increases transmission risk.

distractorRationales.b: The safer response addresses infection progression; "Use alcohol-based sanitizer alone because it is faster" moves care toward diagnostic reassurance before infection or sepsis escalation.
distractorRationales.c: Because c diff spore precautions infection suggests infection progression, "Place the client on droplet precautions only" would not correct the active problem. Infection control or sepsis escalation limits spread and organ hypoperfusion before lower-acuity care.
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses infection progression; "Share the blood pressure cuff after wiping it with a dry towel" moves care toward diagnostic reassurance before infection or sepsis escalation.

questions.takeaway: C. difficile care requires soap-and-water hand hygiene and contact precautions.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.
